title: Is the Robot Being Fair?

modules.title: The Sorting Hat Has a Problem
modules.sub: Meet SORTR and discover why its choices feel wrong
modules.description: You arrive in Pebbletown and watch SORTR make a series of surprising decisions — giving the best books, tables, and roles to the same kinds of kids every time. By exploring three real scenarios, you start collecting clues about what fairness means and why something about SORTR's choices doesn't sit right.

modules.title: Where Did the Machine Learn That?
modules.sub: Trace how SORTR was taught and what it absorbed
modules.description: You dig into SORTR's past to find out where it got its rules — uncovering the data and examples it was trained on. Through a hands-on sorting activity, you see how a machine can accidentally learn unfair patterns if the information it studies is already unbalanced.

modules.title: Spot the Unfair Rule
modules.sub: Hunt for the hidden rules causing SORTR's bad decisions
modules.description: You examine SORTR's decision rules up close and use a detective checklist to label each one as fair, unfair, or unsure. By the end of the module, you can name at least one specific rule that is causing an unfair outcome and explain why it's a problem in your own words.

modules.title: Fix It: Redesign the Game
modules.sub: Rewrite SORTR's rules so every kid gets a fair chance
modules.description: Now it's your turn to be the designer — you propose new rules for SORTR and test them against different kids in Pebbletown to see if outcomes improve. You finish by writing a short "fairness promise" that explains your rule change and why it makes things better for everyone.

synopsis: In the village of Pebbletown, an AI called SORTR decides who gets the good library books, the best lunch table, and the starring role in the school play — and something feels wrong. You play a kid detective who must figure out why SORTR keeps making unfair choices and then redesign its rules so everyone gets a fair chance.
rationale: Young children are already subject to algorithmic decisions in schools and apps but have no vocabulary or mental model for fairness in automated systems, making early concrete exposure essential.
learning_outcome: Students can identify at least one real-world situation where an AI might treat people unfairly, explain in their own words why that happens, and propose one rule change that would make the outcome fairer.
source_signals: 💓 HUMANS VS AI 🤖 | What Is AI ? | Artificial Intelligence Explained | Kids Learning Videos | EMoMee
AI TEACHERS Run The School | Dhar Mann Studios
